role_summary: Poet and French professor at the University of the South (Sewanee), Highlander Folk School board member from 1958, and organizer of the Franklin County, Tennessee NAACP chapter.
ai_reading: Bates was a white anti-war poet whose movement work ran through Highlander and the Episcopal South, yet cosine similarity in this corpus pulls his testimony first toward Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29), SCLC's Education Director, before it reaches the Highlander music figures one might expect. Cotton is his strongest neighbor by a wide margin (his passages match hers in 49 of his 94 indexed chunks, average similarity 0.61), with the joint Candie and Guy Carawan interview (entry 11) a distant second at 25 chunks; the embedding reads his account as citizenship-school pedagogy more than folk-song tradition, even though he spends much of the interview on Highlander's workshops and singing. The second observation is positional: across all five concept axes his coordinates fall within plus or minus 0.04 of center, which makes him the eighth most centrist of the 136 entries on the combined axes and the eleventh most centrist on the Sacred versus Secular Framing axis alone. For an interviewee whose narrative is explicitly Christian-left (Fellowship of Reconciliation, the social gospel, an Episcopal college), the vector signature reads as neither sacred nor secular but balanced between them.
biographical_paragraph: Alfred Scott Bates was born in Evanston, Illinois on June 13, 1923, graduated from Carleton College, and was drafted into the Army in World War II, where a year of intensive French study at the University of Chicago sent him to Normandy and into Military Government in France as an interpreter [src: 1, 2]. He joined the French faculty of the University of the South at Sewanee, Tennessee in 1954, taught French and later film there until his retirement in 1993, and edited the anthology Poems of War Resistance from 2300 B.C. to the Present in 1969 [src: 1, 3]. Bates began attending workshops at the Highlander Folk School at its Grundy County location in 1956 and joined Highlander's board of directors in 1958, a seat he held until his death, becoming an emeritus member in the 1990s [src: 1]. Working with Septima Clark (the school's education director, then organizing citizenship work) and Highlander founder Myles Horton, he helped bring an NAACP chapter to Franklin County, Tennessee in an effort that produced a lawsuit desegregating the county's public schools [src: 1, 2]. In the interview he recounts meeting Esau Jenkins, Andrew Young, and folk musician Guy Carawan through Highlander, and he places himself at the Highlander gathering where SNCC figures convened before the 1960 founding meeting at Shaw University; Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29) and the joint interview of Candie and Guy Hughes Carawan (entry 11) are his nearest neighbors in the corpus. He died in Sewanee on August 25, 2013 [src: 1, 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 116 of the Civil Rights History Project, recorded June 20, 2013 by David P. Cline (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 4].

interview_snippets.speaker: Scott Bates
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked how a French professor from Illinois came to civil rights work in the South, Bates describes the commitment he found after taking the Sewanee job:
interview_snippets.quote: And so I found my life's dedication, baked me to bring in blacks to the south and desegregate the south and to work with Highlander Folk School.

interview_snippets.speaker: Scott Bates
interview_snippets.lead_in: On how the local NAACP chapter he organized got started, and who guided it:
interview_snippets.quote: So I started the chapter under the supervision of Septimaclack.

interview_snippets.speaker: Scott Bates
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recounting a Highlander meeting that preceded SNCC's founding, Bates recalls what Myles Horton told the young organizers in the room:
interview_snippets.quote: Myles said, you know, you guys, you gotta do it yourself. You know, the white's a lot stick with you. And he was right too.

interview_snippets.speaker: Scott Bates
interview_snippets.lead_in: When Black Panther visitors arrived from Chicago carrying firearms, Bates explains the rule that kept the Klan-watched school from being burned:
interview_snippets.quote: And we had a sitting rule, no guns.

interview_snippets.speaker: Scott Bates
interview_snippets.lead_in: On why he still guides visitors to the remains of the original Highlander Folk School near Monteagle:
interview_snippets.quote: The place where really the one spot where the whole movement started, the active movement of it.

gallery.alt: Myles Horton seated on a rock in the Cumberland Mountains near Monteagle, Tennessee, in the 1930s.
gallery.caption: Myles Horton, founder of the Highlander Folk School near Monteagle, Tennessee, photographed in the Cumberland Mountains in the 1930s. Bates joined Highlander's board in 1958 and worked with Horton to organize the Franklin County, Tennessee NAACP chapter; in his oral history he recalls Horton telling young organizers before the 1960 founding of SNCC, 'you gotta do it yourself.'
gallery.repository: Swiss National Library, Bern (Schwarzenbach Archive, SLA-Schwarzenbach-A-5-10-115)

sources.title: In Remembrance of Highlander Board Member Scott Bates
sources.publisher: Highlander Research and Education Center (the institutional successor to the Highlander Folk School, on whose board Bates served from 1958 until his death; records his Highlander workshop attendance from 1956, board tenure, and the Franklin County NAACP and school-desegregation lawsuit with Septima Clark and Myles Horton)

sources.title: Celebrating Scott Bates (June 13, 1923 to August 25, 2013)
